retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, an important cause of ambiguous genitalia in children. Initially, the phenotype of children with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency can vary from underdeveloped male genitalia to fully developed female genitalia. This phenotype may change during puberty, at which point male secondary sex characteristics may develop. This activity will assist practitioners in recognizing when to consider this diagnosis and how to manage it. This activity highlights the critical role of the interprofessional care team in caring for patients with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Sexual Development -- Clinical Significance -- Defects in Androgen Biosynthesis
retrieved_docs.content: 17-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency 5-alpha reductase deficiency

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Dihydrotestosterone -- Clinical Significance -- 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: The patients ultimately develop a male gender identity and a sexual preference for females. These individuals can become fertile with surgery to correct the male ductal system. Female development is largely unimpacted by a congenital 5-alpha-reductase deficiency. Normal female growth and development are not dependent on significant DHT activity. The low DHT levels may lead to reduced body hair growth and a mild decrease in pubic hair.

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the roles of testosterone versus dihydrotestosterone in both male fetal development and puberty. Identify some of the gender identity struggles that children with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency may face when they reach puberty. Determine the testing that should be done if 5-alpha-reductase deficiency is suspected and identify whether additional testing is useful. Communicate how interprofessional team members can collaborate to provide optimal counseling to patients with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency and their families. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Dihydrotestosterone -- Clinical Significance -- 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: The 5-alpha-reductase enzyme is involved in the production of DHT. The enzyme deficiencies are an autosomal recessive condition, typically arising due to loss-of-function mutations in the gene encoding 5-alpha-reductase type 2. [9] Males born with a 5-alpha-reductase deficiency have underdeveloped genitalia, undescended functional testes, and a small or absent prostate. The development of the testes and the internal organs of sexual differentiation are unaltered. The presentation is variable depending on the enzyme level.

retrieved_docs.title: Micropenis -- Treatment / Management -- Gender Assignment in Cases of Micropenis
retrieved_docs.content: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome results in phenotypic females. The consensus is that individuals with this condition should be raised as females despite their 46,XY genotype. [146] [150] The condition is typically diagnosed during evaluation for female infertility. [148] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome ," for more information. Children with 5α-reductase deficiency and micropenis are likely to experience spontaneous penile lengthening at puberty and are therefore raised as males. [146] The enzyme 5α-reductase converts testosterone into the more potent DHT, which is necessary for the early development of the male penis. [146] Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " 5-Alpha Reductase Deficiency " and " Biochemistry, Dihydrotestosterone ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: 5α-RD usually produces severe male genital ambiguity with a bifid scrotum, urogenital sinus, and a clitoris like a phallus. Inadequate endocrine evaluation in newborns may fail to make a correct diagnosis and determine a gender assignment as opposed to genetic and gonadal sex, with possible deterioration in long-term outcomes. [19] It is vital that an interprofessional team that includes a geneticist, pediatrician, endocrinologist, pediatric surgeon, urologist, obstetrician, and gynecologist help determine the appropriate course for the infant. At the same time, as the child gets older, a mental health nurse should provide counseling because some of these children have ambivalent feelings about their gender assignment. The full effort of an interprofessional healthcare team can lead to improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ambiguous genitalia is a very rare problem encountered in newborns, with a prevalence of 1 in 4500 live births. Even with the recent advances in technology like genetic workup, an assay of hormones, and karyotyping, only 20% to 40% of the time can the diagnosis be made in these children. [1] 5-alpha-reductase type 2 (5α-RD2) deficiency is one of the important causes of ambiguous genitalia in children. The phenotype of children with 5α-RD2 deficiency can vary from underdeveloped male genitalia to a complete female phenotype. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ambiguous Genitalia and Disorders of Sexual Differentiation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: 46 XY DSD: For individuals with a deficiency of 17 beta-hydroxy dehydrogenase or deficiency of 5 alpha-reductase, a male gender of rearing is recommended. Hormonal stimulation for phallic growth followed by hypospadias correction and orchidopexy is advocated.

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: 5α-RD2 deficiency is an inherited autosomal recessive disease. The deficiency is due to mutations in the gene that encodes for the enzyme 5α-RD2 on the short arm of chromosome 2. Up to 40 mutations have been reported, leading to this disease. The levels of DHT are low in these children, which affects the sexual differentiation of male external genitalia. Although the genotype of these children is 46 XY, the phenotype varies from case to case. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The parents of the child with ambiguous genitalia have legal rights to seek assistance, support, and any information on the child's problem. They can take adequate time to decide the treatment options and the gender of the child. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis in ambiguous genitalia should be considered after a precise anatomical examination and hormonal assay. The significant differentials that can present similarly to 5α-RD2 deficiency include androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS) and insufficiency of 17 beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 3. [17] [18] Diagnosing 5α-RD2 deficiency and AIS is critical because individuals with 5α-RD2 deficiency are usually raised as males and AIS as females. Sometimes, the phenotype of 5α-RD2 deficiency can be similar to Leydig cell hypoplasia associated with a mutation of LH receptor on the Leydig cells. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: 17-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Sex Hormone Replacement and Surgical Interventions in 46,XY Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Reproductive medicine specialists may consider additional treatment options for fertility, as many of these patients are unable to conceive. Historically, live births in these patients have been rare, but recent cases of successful live births have increased, primarily through assisted reproductive techniques and, in very rare instances, spontaneously after adequate disease control. These successful cases have utilized various protocols, including progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation, long-acting gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist/dexamethasone or prednisolone, short-acting GnRH agonist/dexamethasone, prednisolone, and ultra-long GnRH agonists. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Micropenis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The simultaneous presentation of micropenis and cryptorchidism suggests a diagnosis of congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. [8] [154] Topical DHT may be effective when topical testosterone therapy fails, particularly in patients with 5α-reductase deficiency. [2] [79] [84] [155] Seethe companion StatPearls' resource, " 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Exclusion of Defects in Testosterone Synthesis
retrieved_docs.content: 5-alpha-reductase deficiency: Differentiation of CAIS and PAIS from 5-alpha-reductase is done by analyzing the ratio of testosterone to DHT in serum. In patients with steroid 5-alpha-reductase 2 deficiency, the production of DHT decreases, and the plasma ratio of testosterone to DHT increases. In most girls/boys with PAIS, the production of DHT and the ratio of testosterone to DHT is normal. Women with CAIS may have secondary 5-alpha-reductase deficiency due to a decreased mass of the urogenital tract tissues, which normally produce DHT.

retrieved_docs.title: 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency -- History and Physical -- Gender Identity Problems in Children with 5α-RD2 Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Although some children with this enzyme deficiency are raised as girls, many would change their gender to males at puberty after virilization. There are multiple factors, such as culture and environmental pressures, which can influence the gender changes in these children. Still, the most significant factor responsible for the gender change is the exposure of the child's brain to androgen, including testosterone, and not to the female sex hormone. A brain exposed to androgens develops more masculine behavior and influences the child to identify itself as a male child rather than a female. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1520
retrieved_docs.content: Inosine deficiency, deficiency of sex steroid hormones, deficiency of sulfur, and deficiency of testosterone are all recognized as allowed qualifiers. These qualifiers denote specific deficits—inosine deficiency refers to a lack of the nucleoside inosine, while deficiency of sex steroid hormones and testosterone indicates insufficient levels of hormones that regulate sexual development and function. Deficiency of sulfur highlights a shortfall of this essential element, which is important for numerous biochemical processes. Recognizing these deficiencies as allowed qualifiers helps clinicians identify and categorize metabolic and hormonal insufficiencies that may require targeted evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens are a class of steroid hormones that serve as male sex hormones, and nandrolone is a specific androgenic anabolic steroid within this group, known for its therapeutic use in conditions requiring anabolic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin antagonists can be described in terms of a deficiency, and within this framework both a deficiency of sulfur and a deficiency of testosterone are recognized as permissible qualifiers for characterizing prostaglandin antagonist activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: التحصّي is associated with other male genital disorders, indicating a connection between this condition and various other disorders affecting the male genitalia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Androgeny and the class of compounds known as androgens are both classified as forms of nandrolone, a synthetic anabolic‑androgenic steroid. As variants of nandrolone, they share the characteristic hormonal activity of this compound, which includes promoting protein synthesis, increasing muscle mass, and exerting androgenic effects. Recognizing androgeny and androgens as types of nandrolone helps clarify their pharmacologic profile and informs clinical decisions regarding their therapeutic use, potential side‑effects, and regulatory considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1367
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens and Androgens and female sex hormones in combination with other drugs are both classified as types of androgens and female sex hormones in combination, indicating that therapeutic regimens that combine androgenic agents with female sex hormones can be administered either as a straightforward androgen‑estrogen mixture or as a more complex formulation that also includes additional pharmacologic agents. Recognizing these subcategories helps clinicians tailor hormone therapy to achieve the desired hormonal balance while addressing the need for adjunctive drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens are classified as members of the broader hormone group that includes estrogens and testosterone, reflecting their role as sex steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2534
retrieved_docs.content: ANABOLIC AGENTS FOR SYSTEMIC USE are classified as synthetic androgens, which are man‑made compounds that mimic the biological activity of natural male sex hormones such as testosterone. As synthetic androgens, these agents are administered systemically to produce anabolic effects—including enhanced protein synthesis, muscle growth, and bone density—and are employed in therapeutic settings such as hormone‑deficient states, certain anemias, and muscle‑wasting disorders. Their synthetic nature also confers potential androgenic side effects, necessitating careful medical oversight during use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2446
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride, a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor, may be used to treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا) by reducing the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, which can help shrink benign prostatic hyperplasia and may also have therapeutic effects on prostatic neoplasms. Its potential benefit in managing prostate tumor highlights the importance of hormonal modulation in urologic oncology and informs clinicians when considering pharmacologic options for patients with prostatic growth abnormalities.

Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.content: and medical procedures underscores the interwoven nature of urogenital and female reproductive health and supports a comprehensive, patient‑centered approach to diagnosis, treatment planning, and counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1462
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, because its androgenic activity can exacerbate prostatic enlargement and worsen the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisone is a glucocorticoid hormone that serves as a parent compound for the broader group of adrenal cortex hormones, indicating that adrenal cortex hormones are derived from or related to cortisone. Within the hierarchy of steroid biology, glucocorticoids encompass cortisone as one of their child members. Clinical terminology recognizes several deficiency states as valid qualifiers, including cortisone deficiency, glucocorticoid deficiency, inosine deficiency, sulfur deficiency, and testosterone deficiency. These deficiency designations highlight the importance of adequate levels of each substance for normal physiological function. Additionally, glucocorticoids are classified as a therapeutic agent category, reflecting their established role in pharmacotherapy for a variety of inflammatory and immune‑mediated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 107
retrieved_docs.content: acidifiers, BPH therapies, alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists, testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors, erectile‑dysfunction agents, other urologicals, gynecological anti‑inflammatories, uterotonics, and therapeutic hormones are interrelated through shared therapeutic categories, active ingredients, pharmacologic mechanisms, and physiological effects, supporting coherent, patient‑centered prescribing and pharmacologic understanding.
